subject: Chemistry
title: Carotenoids: Flamingos and Carrots
description: A micro-lesson that makes Carotenoids: Flamingos and Carrots usable.

hook.text: Flamingos are born gray. They turn pink because they eat shrimp full of carotenoid pigments that accumulate in their feathers.

buildup.text: Carotenoids are long-chain molecules with alternating double bonds. This conjugated system absorbs blue-green light.

discovery.text: Beta-carotene in carrots absorbs blue-green light and reflects orange. Your body splits it into retinol for vision.

twist.text: Eat enough carrots and your skin turns orange—a harmless condition called carotenodermia. The pigment deposits in your skin.

climax.text: Autumn leaves turn red from anthocyanins, but the yellow and orange colors were always there—masked by green chlorophyll.

punchline.text: You are what you eat—literally. Flamingos turn pink from shrimp pigments.

quiz.question: Why are flamingos pink?
options: Carotenoid pigments from their shrimp diet accumulate in their feathers
A genetic mutation produces pink melanin in flamingos
Flamingos secrete a pink oil that coats their feathers
